channel.title: Mapping Luhmann's Brain
channel.description: The first complete network analysis of Niklas Luhmann's 73,715-card Zettelkasten. By Martijn Aslander.

item.title: Building a Machine That Reads Luhmann's Handwriting Good Enough
item.description: Six AI models, 73,715 cards, one day. 15.6% error rate. What happens when you stop aiming for perfection and start aiming for useful.

item.title: The Ripple Method — How Context Reads Context
item.description: When AI can't read 45-year-old German handwriting, the surrounding cards can help. A multi-agent system that lets the network read itself. CER from 163% to 27%.

item.title: The Architecture Behind It — How ThetaOS Maps Onto Luhmann
item.description: A 10-layer synaptic model, myelination scores, and an 8-dimensional diamond layer. Why this architecture works on the most famous knowledge system in history.

item.title: 8 Lessons for Anyone Building a Knowledge System
item.description: After mapping 73,715 cards and 18,289 cross-references, eight lessons emerge that apply to anyone building a personal knowledge system.

item.title: What Happens When You Have All 90,000 Cards
item.description: We mapped 58% of Luhmann's Zettelkasten. Here's what becomes possible when we have the rest.

item.title: 50 Things Nobody Knew About Luhmann's Zettelkasten
item.description: The first network analysis of all 73,715 cards reveals surprises about his thinking that 70 years of scholarship missed.

item.title: How I Got 73,715 Cards in a Couple of Minutes
item.description: The hidden API, the download script (80 lines), and why the archive was always open — nobody took it.
